message.content:
## Web Searcher: naming strategy — what makes coined tech brand names ownable and strong

Research question: "Find the best available brand name for a software product, and verify availability for each finalist. 

THE PRODUCT: an autonomous "deep research" AI agent/pipeline. It fans out web + academic searches across many sources, then writes a long-form research report (e.g. literature reviews, market/industry analyses) in which EVERY claim is traced back to a real, verifiable citation. Its core differentiator vs competitors is FAITHFULNESS and RIGOR — a built-in verifier repairs/re-grounds each sentence to source evidence so the report does not hallucinate. Think of it as "a research analyst that never makes up a fact." Positioning themes to draw from: truth / veracity / faithfulness / proof, depth / synthesis / research, insight / clarity / illumination, and (secondarily) autonomous agentic speed.

CURRENT NAME TO REPLACE: "Polaris" (rejected — too generic, and heavily trademarked: Polaris Inc. snowmobiles, Polaris missiles, many software products). AVOID this and the whole cliché family of star/compass/navigation/"north star" names.

NAMING STYLE: open — prefer short (2-3 syllables), pronounceable, brandable names. Coined/abstract words (like Vercel, Cohere, Anthropic, Kadoa) are welcome because they are the most ownable; obscure real words or Greek/Latin/other-language roots with a truth/insight meaning are also welcome. Avoid the crowded, already-taken names in the AI-research space — explicitly check and EXCLUDE names already used by: Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Undermind, Scite, You.com, Genspark, Storm/STORM (Stanford), Ai2/OpenScholar, Scholarcy, Semantic Scholar, Deep Research (OpenAI/Google/Perplexity feature names), Glean, Hebbia, Vespa, and any obvious near-duplicates.

DELIVERABLE: a RANKED shortlist of 8-12 candidate names. For EACH candidate provide:
  1. The name, its pronunciation, and a 1-2 sentence meaning/rationale tying it to the truth/rigor/deep-research positioning.
  2. GitHub availability: is the org/username taken? (check github.com/<name>).
  3. Trademark risk: search USPTO TESS and EUIPO for live marks in the software/AI classes (Nice class 9 and 42); flag any conflict, note if clear.
  4. Domain availability: is <name>.ai and/or <name>.com registered/parked/available? Note approximate acquisition feasibility.
  5. Social handle: is @<name> (or a close variant) free on X/Twitter?
  6. A risk flag: GREEN (all clear), YELLOW (minor/partial conflict), or RED (real conflict) with one-line reason.
Then give a clear TOP RECOMMENDATION (1-2 names) that best balances meaning + full availability across all four channels, and explain why. Prioritize candidates that are GREEN on ALL of: GitHub org, trademark, at least one of .ai/.com, and a social handle. It is better to return fewer names that are genuinely fully-available than many that are taken."

Your angle: **naming strategy — what makes coined tech brand names ownable and strong** — Grounds the ranking criteria — which naming patterns are most defensible/ownable, easiest to trademark, and most pronounceable — so the shortlist and top pick are principled, not arbitrary.
Search query: `coined abstract company name Vercel Cohere Anthropic why brandable ownable naming best practices pronounceable`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on my search results, I can now provide you with comprehensive information about coined abstract company names and branding best practices:

## Key Findings on Coined Abstract Company Names and Naming Best Practices

### What Are Coined Abstract Names?

Fanciful names are made-up words created solely to serve as unique brand identifiers. Unlike descriptive names, they convey no direct meaning or qualities and are often whimsical, creative, and easily trademarked.

Coined names are freshly created terms with no prior meaning, like "Kodak." These unique and memorable names often stand out beautifully in crowded markets.

### Why Brandable and Ownable Names Matter

With an abstract brand name, you're creating an entirely new word from scratch. This means you're not likely to end up with something similar to an existing title. You can have complete ownership over the title you create, and usually, you'll be able to trademark your moniker too, so it's easier to protect.

Abstract names can be more distinctive and easier to trademark, but descriptive names are often easier to understand immediately.

### Best Practices for Pronounceability

The point is to make the spelling and phonetics friendly, so that it does not cause problems when it comes to reading or pronouncing it.

Pronunciation matters too. If your name requires explanation across cultures, it'll slow your growth.

Choosing a name that is difficult to spell or pronounce is perhaps the most common and most damaging mistake. If people cannot spell your name after hearing it, they cannot find you online. If they cannot pronounce it after reading it, they will not recommend you to others through word of mouth.

### Phonetic Design Principles

Abstract names usually leverage a variety of different sounds in a short term to create a modern, futuristic experience. This is excellent for a company looking to create a future-proof and innovative brand identity.

The strategic use of vowels in brand names is essential for creating memorable and pronounceable identities. The examples provided illustrate how successful companies have embraced this principle, resulting in brand names that resonate with consumers. When crafting a brand name, consider the placement of vowels to enhance its appeal and effectiveness.

### Broader Naming Strategy Considerations

Invented names also work globally without translation issues, making them ideal for companies with international ambitions. They're particularly effective for tech innovators and companies creating entirely new categories, where you need maximum distinctiveness to stand out.

However, there's a trade-off: Invented names require significant marketing investment to build meaning. Since they have no inherent meaning, you must create associations through consistent brand marketing and storytelling.

The search results did not contain specific information about the meaning or origin of the names Vercel, Cohere, or Anthropic themselves, though they are used as examples in the broader context of tech company naming.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: I have enough to rank the results by relevance to the naming-strategy angle. Let me capture the most useful sources.

results.title: What is abstract naming? The pros and cons of abstract brand names (Fabrik Brands)
results.snippet: Directly grounds the ranking criteria: coined/abstract names are the most ownable and trademarkable because you invent a new word unlikely to collide with existing marks. Also flags the trade-off — no inherent meaning, so associations must be built through marketing. Best single source for WHY coined names (Vercel/Cohere/Anthropic-style) are defensible.

results.title: Why Vowels Matter in Brand Names (Dozro)
results.snippet: Phonetic-design principle backing the pronounceability requirement in the shortlist. Argues vowel placement drives memorability and pronounceability — the mechanism behind why short 2-3 syllable coined names read cleanly. Useful for scoring how 'sayable' each finalist is.

results.title: Making the right choice: what should I name my tech company? (Structure)
results.snippet: Branding-agency framework for tech naming that stresses global pronounceability and distinctiveness for category-creating products — relevant since the deep-research agent is defining a rigor/faithfulness niche. Reinforces choosing maximally distinctive coined marks over descriptive ones.

results.title: Ultimate Guide to Brand Naming: Create Memorable Names (Spellbrand)
results.snippet: Warns that hard-to-spell/pronounce names are the most damaging naming mistake — if people can't spell it after hearing it they can't find you, and can't recommend it by word of mouth. Practical screen for eliminating finalists that fail the 'say it, spell it, find it' test.

results.title: Why Abstract Names Are Overused and Underperform (NamingForce)
results.snippet: Counterpoint that keeps the criteria honest: abstract names carry no built-in meaning and can underperform without heavy brand investment. Supports favoring coined names that at least carry a phonetic hint of truth/insight (semi-abstract) over pure gibberish for a positioning-heavy product.

results.title: Abstract business names – do unique brand names really work? (Dotinum)
results.snippet: Reiterates the ownership advantage — inventing a word from scratch means you won't collide with existing titles and can usually trademark and protect it. Corroborates the trademark-defensibility rationale for prioritizing coined candidates in the shortlist.
